results.possibleIdentity: Andrew Ian Porter (also known as Andy Porter) is an American editor and publisher who was a prominent figure in the New York science fiction and trade publishing scenes starting in the 1960s. In the late 1960s, he served as an associate editor at Lancer Books, a New York-based paperback publisher. During this same period, he was the assistant editor for The Magazine of Fantasy & Science Fiction (1966–1974). Porter's career also included significant work in technical and industrial trade publishing; he worked as a trade magazine editor and advertising production manager for titles such as Electro-Procurement, Construction Equipment, Rudder, and Quick Frozen Foods. He is widely recognized in the science fiction community for founding and editing the Hugo Award-winning fanzine Algol (later Starship) and the long-running professional newszine Science Fiction Chronicle. His involvement in the New York technical and artistic circles of the late 1960s, combined with his role as a production manager and editor for technical journals, aligns with the historical profile of an editor active in the New York area with a technical background. While he is most famous for his work in science fiction, his professional activities in trade publishing (including the electronics industry via Electro-Procurement) occurred during the era when the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization was active in NYC.
